sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Temporomandibular and jaw musculoskeletal pain can reflect myofascial overload, bruxism, joint irritation, or referred cervical symptoms. Conservative care is common, but dental, neurological, infectious, or fracture causes must be screened.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Jaw pain with chewing, yawning, or clenching
- Clicking, locking, or reduced mouth opening
- Associated headache, facial ache, or neck tension
- Morning stiffness or symptom flare with stress or prolonged clenching

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for trauma, dislocation, severe trismus, fever, dental sepsis, cranial nerve findings, or unexplained weight loss
- Bruxism, stress, and cervical loading often coexist with TMD symptoms
- Persistent locking or major bite change warrants specialist review

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen mouth opening, trauma, dental red flags, and whether the patient can eat, drink, and speak safely
- Advise relative unloading, softer diet, jaw relaxation, and simple symptom relief within scope
- Escalate for infection, dislocation, or major functional compromise

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess jaw opening, deviation, tenderness, cervical contribution, and symptom triggers
- Use education, self-management, and graded jaw and cervical exercises rather than forceful stretching
- Refer for dental, maxillofacial, or medical review when locking, severe bite change, or persistent severe pain remains

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Jaw pain often improves when clenching load, sleep, stress, and cervical contributors are addressed together
